coredata.dc:title: Decoration of carbon dots on S-scheme TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10 heterojunction photocatalyst: Impressive QDs-sized photocatalyst for efficient degradation of common antibiotics and organic pollutants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Moslemi-Eilanlou, Maryam
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Salmanzadeh-Jamadi, Zahra
Khataee, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: The effectiveness of photocatalytic materials strongly depends on factors such as stability, light absorption range, charge transfer efficiency, and biocompatibility. Therefore, in the present work, Ti3 + self-doped/oxygen vacancies (Ti3+/OVs) TiO2 was integrated with Bi24O31Br10 and carbon dots and employed for degradation of various organic pollutants (malachite green: MG, methylene blue: MB, and rhodamine B: RhB dyes, tetracycline hydrochloride: TC and amoxicillin: AMX antibiotics). The light-induced charge separation pathway was evaluated through free radical trapping experiments, and it was confirmed that the TiO2-x, Bi24O31Br10, and carbon dots components collaborated through S-scheme charge transfer pathway. Under the optimal reaction conditions, the TC degradation rate over TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10/carbon dots-2 nanocomposite reached 98 % within 30 min, and the reaction rate constant was 1280 × 10 4 min 1, which was 9.70 times of Bi24O31Br10, 5.64 folds of TiO2-x, and 2.05 folds of TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalysts. Such outstanding performance was attributed to the optimal anchoring of Bi24O31Br10 and C-Dots on the surface of TOVs, the quantum dot size of the resultant photocatalyst, increased surface area, low charge transmission resistance, the effective presence of carbon dots with mediator properties, and most importantly the construction of a S-scheme system which suppressed e–/h+ pair recombination, as confirmed by EIS and PL analyses. The stability and biocompatibility of the TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10/carbon dots-2 nanocomposite were confirmed by reusing five times and growing wheat seeds in the treated solution, respectively. This research has presented a novel nanocomposite that can be utilized in dye degradation and antibiotic removal with the aim of plants irrigation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10/carbon dots
Wastewater decontamination
S-scheme heterojunction
Visible-light-induced photocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the materials.

(a-c) FESEM images of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposites using varying amounts of carbon dots (1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mL), (d) TEM and particle distribution diagram, (e) HRTEM, and (f) SAED pattern for TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite.
(a) EDX spectrum and (b-h) EDX mapping of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite.
The XPS spectra of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite: Survey (a), Ti 2p (b), Br 3d (c), Bi 4 f (d), O 1 s (e), and C 1 s (f).
(a) Photodegradation of TC, (b) Kinetic plots for TC degradation over different photocatalysts, and (c) Degradation rate constants of the studied systems.

(a) Role of scavengers in the photocatalytic degradation of TC over TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite, (b) The PL spectra of terephthalic acid for •OH trapping in the presence of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite, (c) The degradation of different pollutants on the photocatalysts, and (d) the TOC removal rates of TC over TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite.
(a, b) BET isotherms and BJH curves for Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 samples, (c) EIS data, and (d) PL spectra for the selected photocatalysts.
(a, b) UV−Vis absorption spectra and Tauc’s plots for the selected photocatalysts, and (c) Mott-Schottky plot for Bi24O31Br10 sample.

(a) Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposite with S-scheme heterojunction structure and (b) analysis of degradation intermediates of TCH over the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposite using LC–MS.
The phytotoxicity tests through growth of wheat seeds.
Cyclic stability of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite for 5 repeated TC photodegradation studies.
Schematic presentation for fabrication of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C photocatalysts.
First-order rate constants, correlation constant, and degradation percentages within 30 min for TC over the prepared materials.
Comparison of the TC photodegradation efficiency for some of the photocatalysts recently prepared based on TOVs and Bi24O31Br10.
Textural characteristics of TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Decoration of carbon dots on S-scheme TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10 heterojunction photocatalyst: Impressive QDs-sized photocatalyst for efficient degradation of common antibiotics and organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil 56199−13131, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, 56199−13131, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 51666−16471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, 51666−16471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, Istanbul 34469, Türkiye

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, 34469 Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effectiveness of photocatalytic materials strongly depends on factors such as stability, light absorption range, charge transfer efficiency, and biocompatibility. Therefore, in the present work, Ti3 + self-doped/oxygen vacancies (Ti3+/OVs) TiO2 was integrated with Bi24O31Br10 and carbon dots and employed for degradation of various organic pollutants (malachite green: MG, methylene blue: MB, and rhodamine B: RhB dyes, tetracycline hydrochloride: TC and amoxicillin: AMX antibiotics). The light-induced charge separation pathway was evaluated through free radical trapping experiments, and it was confirmed that the TiO2-x, Bi24O31Br10, and carbon dots components collaborated through S-scheme charge transfer pathway. Under the optimal reaction conditions, the TC degradation rate over TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10/carbon dots-2 nanocomposite reached 98 % within 30 min, and the reaction rate constant was 1280 × 10 4 min 1, which was 9.70 times of Bi24O31Br10, 5.64 folds of TiO2-x, and 2.05 folds of TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalysts. Such outstanding performance was attributed to the optimal anchoring of Bi24O31Br10 and C-Dots on the surface of TOVs, the quantum dot size of the resultant photocatalyst, increased surface area, low charge transmission resistance, the effective presence of carbon dots with mediator properties, and most importantly the construction of a S-scheme system which suppressed e–/h+ pair recombination, as confirmed by EIS and PL analyses. The stability and biocompatibility of the TiO2-x/Bi24O31Br10/carbon dots-2 nanocomposite were confirmed by reusing five times and growing wheat seeds in the treated solution, respectively. This research has presented a novel nanocomposite that can be utilized in dye degradation and antibiotic removal with the aim of plants irrigation.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past half a century, the industrialization and the excessive growth of the population have increased the production of complex and highly stable chemicals, which are directly entered the environment. Several toxic and stable compounds cannot be successfully removed from the effluents, which cause pollution of the food chain and the spread of various diseases [1–3]. Therefore, to clean these harmful pollutants from the environment, researchers are looking for effective, cheap, and environmentally friendly methods [4]. So far, physical, biological, and chemical methods have been used for wastewater treatment [5]. Among the chemical methods of water purification, we can refer to advanced oxidation processes (AOPs). In these processes, active species and radicals such as •O2 −, •OH, and h+ are produced, which are subsequently utilized for oxidation of pollutant molecules and convert them into smaller and less dangerous molecules [6,7]. Heterogeneous photocatalysts, as one of the promising AOPs, can convert solar energy to produce active species for wastewater remediation [8,9]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has received much attention in the field of solar cells and photocatalytic technology, due to its high resistance to photo-corrosion, more chemical resistance, non-toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [10–14]. Nonetheless, its real-world usage is significantly constrained because of the substantial energy gap and the elevated recombination rates of charge carriers [10–15]. Among the various strategies employed to enhance the photocatalytic ability of TiO2, defect engineering has received special attention from researchers due to the creation of mid gap states through generation of oxygen vacancies (OV) and Ti+3 in the TiO2 structure [16–18]. The developed defects not only improve visible-light-absorption ability, but also suppresses the charges recombination of the pristine TiO2. The enhanced absorption of visible light by defective TiO2-x (abbreviated as TOVs) significantly boosts the generation of e–/h+ pairs, leading to an improvement in photocatalytic efficiency [19]. An additional essential aspect influencing the photocatalytic performance is the catalyst's surface area. The defect engineering within TiO2 enhances its surface area to ∼200 m²/g, while that of pristine TiO2 is only ∼63 m²/g [20]. Despite the increased photocatalytic activity of TOVs compared to TiO2, due to the fast recombination of charges, its activity is far from the industrial-scale demand. Consequently, integration of TOVs with different semiconductors having appropriate energy bands can lead to fabrication of efficacious photocatalysts [21,22].

Recently, Bi24O31Br10 with a lower Br/O ratio with a layered structure, favorable energy gap of about 2.7 eV, and internal electrostatic field (IEF) perpendicular to each layer has been recognized as a new class of photocatalysts [23,24]. Furthermore, carbon dots (denoted as C), as modifying materials and a fascinating class of discrete quasi-spherical carbon particles (<10 nm), are of particular interest as mediators with excellent upconversion photoluminescence properties [25,26].

Herein, the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C heterostructured photocatalysts were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal route. To ensure the effectiveness of the hydrothermal method for preparing the desired heterojunction, a set of characteristics such as XPS, EDS, HRTEM, SAED, FT-IR, and XRD were used. Moreover, UV–vis DRS, PL, EIS, BET, and BJH analyses were employed to examine the optical properties, charge recombination dynamics, charge transfer resistance, surface area, and pore volume of the photocatalysts. The performance of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C photocatalysts was superior compared with the TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 in terms of visible-light photocatalysis for tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), amoxicillin (AMX), malachite green (MG), methylene blue (MB), and rhodamine B (RhB) degradations. In addition, the charge migration path was studied by free radical trapping experiments. Most importantly, the biocompatibility of the treated solution was evaluated with wheat seed culture.

The reagents employed in this study were bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O, Lobachemie, 98 %), nitric acid (HNO3, Lobachemie, 69 %), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, Kimia alcohol Zanjan, 99.8 %), potassium bromide (KBr, Merck, 99.5 %), titanium (IV) butoxide (Ti (OBut)4, 97 %, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, Lobachemie, 99 %), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, Lobachemie, 99.5 %), urea (CO(NH2)2, Lobachem, 99 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, Merck, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Merck, 99 %), TC and AMX (Sigma-Aldrich), MG, MB, and RhB (Lobachemie).

The synthesis process of the TOVs has been presented in the previous work [27]. Moreover, a hydrothermal method was used to prepare the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalyst. In the first step, ultrasonic waves were used to disperse TOVs powder (0.4 g) in water (100 mL). In the following, 0.185 g Bi(NO3)3.5H2O was added to the suspension after being dissolved in ethylene glycol (10 mL), and magnetically stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, a KBr solution (0.02 g dissolved in 10 mL H2O) was gradually introduced into the previous mixture, and the stirring was maintained for 30 min. Next, the process of adding 3 M NaOH solution to the final suspension was carried until the suspension pH reached 10. The resulting suspension was placed into an autoclave to undergo a hydrothermal reaction at a temperature of 160 °C for a duration of 12 hours. Following the synthesis, the resulting material underwent centrifugation, and rinsed with water and ethanol, and then dried. The carbon dots solution was prepared using the reported procedures [28]. For synthesis of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposite, we first dispersed 0.4 g of the synthesized TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalyst and different amounts of carbon dots (1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mL) within 100 mL of water for 30 min under ultrasonic waves, and finally dried and the dried powder was labeled as TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2, and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3, respectively, based on the amount of added carbon dots solution ( Scheme 1).

The XRD data for the synthesized materials were obtained using a Philips Xpert diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The structures of the synthesized optimal photocatalyst were analyzed using an FE-SEM (MIRA3 FEG) and a TEM (Thermo Scientific Talos F200S) instrument. The XPS (Flex multi spectrometer) analysis was performed with an Al Kα X-ray source. To analyze the optical characteristics of photocatalysts, a Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer was utilized. The textural characteristics were measured by the N2 adsorption/desorption process in a BELSORP mini II instrument. The PerkinElmer Spectrum RX I instrument was employed to conduct FT-IR analysis. The prepared photocatalysts were subjected to electrochemical impedance (EIS, μAutolab III/FRA2) and photoluminescence (PL, Cary Eclipse) analyses to obtain more information about separation of electron/hole pairs. The total organic carbon (TOC) of the reaction system was assessed using a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu TOC-LCPH). The potential degradation pathways of TC were analyzed using an LC-MS system (QTRAP Enabled Triple Quad 5500 +).

Photocatalysis experiments were carried out in a cylindrical Pyrex reactor with a capacity of approximately 400 mL. To maintain a constant temperature of 25 °C, the reactor was equipped with a water circulation system. The reaction mixture was stirred using a magnetic stirrer, while a continuous aeration pump ensured oxygen supply and promoted thorough mixing. A 50 W LED lamp served as the visible light source. This light source was placed approximately 20 cm above the liquid surface. The photocatalyst was dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 6 min. The suspension containing 100 mg of photocatalyst and 200 mL of the aqueous solution of the pollutant was continuously stirred in the dark for 60 min before lighting to achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium. Following a 60 min dark condition, the light source was activated, and samples were collected at predetermined times. Subsequently, a UV-Vis spectrophotometer was employed to quantify the residual concentration of TC, AMX, MG, MB, and RhB by measuring absorbance at wavelengths of 365, 226, 610, 664, and 553 nm, respectively [27,28].

The XRD analysis was used to observe and check the crystal structure of the fabricated materials. In this regard, the XRD patterns of Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1a. In the pattern of Bi24O31Br10, all corresponding peaks were identified [29]. The XRD patterns of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalysts comprise tetragonal TOVs (JCPDS 00–004–0477) [27] and monoclinic Bi24O31Br10 (JCPDS 01–075–0888) components [29]. In the case of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst, the peak of the carbon dots component was not observed due to its minimal amount. The chemical bonds in Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalysts were studied using FTIR analysis (Fig. 1b). The Bi–O (550 cm− 1) [27], Ti–O–Ti (500–800 cm− 1) [28], Bi–Br (1400 cm− 1) [30], C–O (1092 cm−1) [28], and O–H vibrations (1600–1640/3300–3500 cm−1) [27] were found to be the critical functional groups within the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst.

The FESEM, TEM, and HR-TEM characterization techniques were used to study the morphology and microstructure of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst. In Fig. 2(a–c), the FESEM images of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposites using varying amounts of carbon dots (1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mL) are presented. As is clear, all three samples have a spherical-like morphology with a small diameter. The TEM analysis was utilized to reveal detailed microstructural characteristics of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst at high magnification (Fig. 2d). The size distribution graph of nanoparticles was derived from the TEM image, indicating that these nanoparticles fall within quantum dot dimensions with an average size of 7.50 nm (inside Fig. 2d). It is worth mentioning that the much smaller sized spherical structures result in increased surface area, which provides more active sites for adsorption of contaminant molecules and generation of more reactive species. The developed heterojunction among TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, and C-Dots components can be seen within the HRTEM image (Fig. 2e). As observed, the lattice spacing of 1.26 Å, 2.81 Å, and 3.20 Å are corresponding to (215) crystal plane in 2θ = 75.0º of TOVs [22], (117) crystal plane in 2θ = 30.5º of Bi24O31Br10 [24], and (002) crystal plane in 2θ = 27.4º of C-Dots [28]. The SAED pattern for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst is presented in Fig. 2f. The observed diffraction rings correspond to the (517)/(117), and (215) planes of bare Bi24O31Br10 and TOVs, respectively. Therefore, the findings of XRD and HRTEM analyses are supported [22,31].

The elemental analyses were conducted on the FESEM instrument and the outcomes were depicted in Fig. 3. As seen in the EDS spectrum in Fig. 3a, Ti, Br, Bi, C, and O elements with respectively 39.25, 1.25, 18.89, 7.81, and 32.80 wt% are existed. Additionally, the EDS mapping of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst revealed a consistent distribution of elements across the nanocomposite, indicating the high purity of the synthesized photocatalyst (Fig. 3(bh)).

An XPS apparatus was employed to analyze the chemical oxidation states and the elemental composition in the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst. An examination of the surface of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst through a survey scan reveals the presence of Ti, O, Bi, Br, and C elements, and no impurity elements were detected ( Fig. 4a). The peaks shown in Fig. 4b are associated with the Ti 2p electrons in + 3 and + 4 oxidation states. Specifically, the peaks at 467.6 and 461.8 eV correspond to the Ti4+ 2p1/2 and Ti4+ 2p3/2 energy levels, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 465.5 and 457.1 eV are associated with the Ti3+ 2p1/2 and Ti3+ 2p3/2 states [27]. Moreover, the peaks at 67.1 and 69.1 eV belong to the Br atoms within the photocatalyst (Fig. 4c). These energies correspond to the Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 energy levels, thereby confirming the presence of Br− in the photocatalyst [32]. The binding energies observed at approximately 157.2 and 162.2 eV correspond to the Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 energy levels, respectively (Fig. 4d) [22]. The O 1 s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks at 527.8, 530.0, and 531.9 eV, which correspond to Ti4+–O, Ti3+–O, and Ti–OH, respectively (Fig. 4e) [33,34]. The C 1 s XPS spectrum of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst can be broken down into six distinct groups of C–H at 282.0, CC (sp2) at 283.0, Ti–O–C at 283.9, C–OH/C–O–C at 286.0, CO at 287.8, and O–CO at 289.2 eV [35–37].

The effectiveness of Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10, and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C photocatalysts for oxidation of TC upon visible light was studied, as presented in Fig. 5a. The experiment conducted in the absence of visible light demonstrated that the concentration of TC remained broadly stable. It was also shown in the previous work that the TC concentration is constant in the absence of photocatalyst [27]. Among all the photocatalysts, the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite exhibited superior performance for degradation of TC (98 %) after 30 min, when compared to the TOVs (45 %) [27], Bi24O31Br10 (47 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (10 %) (74 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) (84 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (30 %) (64 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1 (92 %), and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3 (86 %) photocatalysts. According to Fig. 5b, it was found that all the prepared materials showed a first-order reaction kinetics. The calculated rate constants for degradation of TC over the photocatalysts along with the corresponding R2 are shown in Table 1. The order of photocatalytic performance is as TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 > TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1 > TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3 > TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) > TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (10 %) > TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (30 %) > TOVs > Bi24O31Br10. An examination of the provided data revealed that the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst exhibited a higher rate for degradation of TC, which was 9.70 times of Bi24O31Br10, 5.64 folds of TOVs, and 2.05 folds of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalysts (Fig. 5c).

To investigate how active radical species effects on the degradation of TC, the scavenging tests were conducted. Chemical quenchers including ammonium oxalate (AO), 2-propanol (2-PrOH), and benzoquinone (BQ)/ascorbic acid (AsA) were added to the reaction system with a concentration of one m mol. L−1 during the photocatalytic processes to elucidate the roles of hole (h+), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide anion radical (•O2 –), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, without any trapping agent, the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst showed the most effective degradation rate. Nevertheless, the presence of these scavengers in the degradation system significantly reduced the efficiency of the degradation reaction. This observation confirmed that all three radical species are responsible for the TC degradation, and the order of action of the species is as •O2 – > h+ > •OH. It has well established that photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy functions as a method for tracking the production of •OH radicals, which are known to be strong oxidizing agents in the oxidation of different organic substances [38]. To study this phenomenon, the experiments were conducted using terephthalic acid tests. Fig. 6b shows the PL spectra with a prominent emission peak at 420 nm, which gradually increases with time. This trend indicates the increase in the concentration of hydroxyl radicals in the solution. As a result, it can be concluded that •OH plays a vital role as the main active species in the photodegradation process of pollutants. In Fig. 6c, the effectiveness of the synthesized photocatalysts in the degradation of different contaminants was investigated. This evaluation was done with promising results observed in the TC degradation using prepared photocatalysts. The results showed that the removal rate constant of MB, RhB, MG, TC, and AMX by the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst were 256 × 10 4, 159 × 10 4, 196 × 10 4, 1280 × 10 4, and 454 × 10 4 min−1, respectively, which were 3.21, 1.36, 1.98, 9.70, and 2.85 times superior to Bi24O31Br10, and 2.00, 1.47, 1.77, 5.64, and 2.72 folds of TOVs, respectively. The findings indicated that the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst demonstrated the most effective degradation rate for pollutants such as MB, RhB, MG, TC, and AMX compared to all other samples. The TOC analysis during the degradation reaction of TC on the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst provided a thorough assessment of its degradation ability, resulting in a TOC removal of about 95 % after only 90 min, which shows that this pollutant is almost completely destroyed during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 6d). These results emphasize the potential of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst as an effective photocatalyst to deal with various pollutants for environmental cleanup. Table 2 presents the photocatalytic performance of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst in comparison with the recent related studies for the degradation of tetracycline antibiotics. The evidence shows the superiority of the prepared photocatalyst compared to other studies under low intensity light irradiation and at shorter reaction time [39–45].

The BET/BJH analyses were performed to explore the textural properties of the TOVs [27] and the photocatalysts mentioned in Fig. 7(a, b) and Table 3. The nitrogen sorption curve shows type IV isotherm for TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite. The results show that the BET specific surface area is 201.9 m2·g−1 for TOVs [27] and 35.42 m2·g−1 for Bi24O31Br10. After the heterojunction developing among TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, and carbon dots, the surface area reached 63.30 m2·g−1 for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) and 102.4 m2·g−1 for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 samples. Increasing the pore volume and specific surface area for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 compared with the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) nanocomposite allows for greater accessibility of active sites, boosting the adsorption of reactants and production of reactive species. This ultimately leads to an improvement in photocatalytic efficiency. The EIS spectra of the photocatalysts showed that TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite has the lowest arc radius compared to TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (10 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (30 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1, and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3 samples (Fig. 7c). As the arc radius decreases, the charge transfer impedance diminishes, resulting in an enhanced transportability of the photogenerated carriers [46]. Hence, it is confirmed that rapid surface charge transfer occurs after developing junctions between TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, and carbon dot components. Furthermore, PL spectroscopy was used to elucidate the better separation of charge carriers generated under visible light on the desired photocatalysts. According to the literature, a lower PL intensity indicates reduced recombination of charge carriers in the samples, which enhances photocatalytic performance [47]. According to Fig. 7d, the intensity of the PL spectra of the samples is as TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 < TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1 < TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3 < TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) < TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (10 %) < TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (30 %) < TOVs < Bi24O31Br10. Hence, the lowest intensity of the PL spectra among the listed samples is that of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite, which indicates a significant decrease in the e−/h+ pair recombination rate.

The solid-state UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra for the as-fabricated materials are presented in Fig. 8a. Both TOVs and Bi24O31Br10 samples show powerful absorption in the visible range. As described in previous work, the vacancy created in the crystal lattice of the TOVs sample significantly broadens the absorption in the visible range [27]. After adding different amounts of Bi24O31Br10 to the TOVs nanospheres, the visible light absorption ability of these materials increased significantly, particularly for the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) photocatalyst. Additionally, the visible-light absorption edge increased dramatically by adding varying amounts of carbon dots to the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %) sample. The band gap energy of the prepared samples was determined by the following equation: (αℎv) 1/n = A (ℎv − Eg) [48]. According to the literature, the value of n is fixed as 1/2 for all synthesized samples according to their direct transition property [44]. The Eg values of TOVs, Bi24O31Br10, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (10 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (20 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10 (30 %), TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-1, TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2, and TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-3 samples are 2.90 [27], 2.80, 2.87, 2.78, 2.75, 1.60, 1.50, and 1.40 eV, respectively (Fig. 8b). The conduction band (CB) positions of TOVs [27] and Bi24O31Br10 samples were obtained by Mott-Schottky measurements. As shown in Fig. 8c and reported in the previous work [27], the flat band potentials (Efb) of TOVs and Bi24O31Br10 were equal to –0.04 and −0.57 V/NHE. The TOVs and Bi24O31Br10 samples are classified as n-type semiconductors [24,27]. In these materials, the CB potential is 0.1 eV more negative than Efb [49]. Therefore, the ECB of TOVs and Bi24O31Br10 samples will be – 0.14 and − 0.67 V, respectively. Based on the formula EVB= ECB + Eg, the calculated EVB values for the TOVs and Bi24O31Br10 samples are + 3.06 and + 2.13 V, respectively.

After calculating the band energies of the mentioned semiconductors, the charge migration path among the counterparts of the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposites under visible light was suggested. The CB and VB positions in the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposites indicate that type-II heterojunctions may exist between the components. The system facilitates charge separation through the movement of electrons from the Bi24O31Br10 to the TOVs and subsequent hole transfer in the opposite direction. However, this process diminishes the effective reduction and oxidation potentials for respectively electrons and holes. However, the findings from the fluorescence emission intensity and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy indicate that within the nanocomposite, there is significant charge separation, transfer, and migration. Also, the results of using electron and hole trapping agents showed that both are the main active species in the photodegradation process. Therefore, according to the results of electrochemical analysis and electron trapping analysis, we can expect the formation of an S-scheme heterojunction between the components. When TOVs semiconductor forms junction with Bi24O31Br10, an automatic migration of electrons takes place from the Fermi level of Bi24O31Br10 to that of TOVs, until their Fermi levels equilibrate ( Fig. 9a). This electron transfer leads to the accumulation of positive and negative charges respectively at the interface of Bi24O31Br10 and TOVs and also creates an internal electric field (IEF) directed from Bi24O31Br10 to TOVs. Subsequently, the Bi24O31Br10 band edges bend upwards due to electron loss and conversely TOVs band edges bend downwards due to electron gain. In this case, the electrons in the CBTOVs combine with the holes accumulated in the VBBi24O31Br10 with the help of CDs, as mediators. As a result, on the developed S-scheme TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C photocatalysts, the excited electrons and holes can be effectively retained in the CBBi24O31Br10 and VBTOVs with respectively high reduction and oxidation abilities to participate in the photocatalytic processes to produce significant number of •O2 –, •OH, and holes. Ultimately, the remarkable photocatalytic activity of TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C nanocomposites is due to the effective charge segregation through an S-scheme heterojunction. This charges transfer pathway effectively increases the transfer rate of charge carriers produced during the photocatalytic process and decreases the recombination rate of charge carriers, as well as leaving numerous e–/h+ pairs available for degradation reactions, which produce significant number of reactive species to attach pollutant molecules. The degradation pathway of TC was examined using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), as a highly effective method for studying the photodegradation reaction. As shown in Fig. 9b, ten intermediates were produced during the photocatalytic reaction. By analyzing the m/z values and consulting relevant literature, the degradation of TC can be categorized into two potential pathways. The results show that the TCH molecule (m/z = 445) undergoes fragmentation into smaller species with m/z values of 395, 378, 376, 290, 224, 217, 206, and 195. The reaction pathway progresses as these intermediates are converted into low molecular weight compounds, including m/z = 132 and m/z = 99. These species then enter the final degradation stages, where mineralization processes completely oxidize them into stable end products such as H₂O, CO₂, NH4 +, and NO3 -.

The biocompatibility of the treated TC solution over the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 nanocomposite was proven by observing the step-by-step growth of wheat grains. As illustrated in Fig. 10, the growth of wheat seeds has been studied under three stages with completely identical environmental conditions under different irrigations. The digital photographs captured after 3, 7, and 12 days indicated that the growth observed in the treated solution was comparable to that of tap water, which served as the control sample. Conversely, the TC solution exhibited reduced growth compared to the control experiment. Consequently, it is confirmed that the TC solution treated by the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst is biocompatible, and it can be used to irrigate plants that do not play any role in the food chain.

The durability and reusability of a photocatalyst play a vital role in evaluating its real-scale application. As shown in Fig. 11, compared to the first run, the degradation efficiency of TC with the optimized photocatalyst reached 91 % after five cycles, which indicates the stability of this photocatalyst for long-term use.

S-scheme QDs-sized TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C photocatalysts with intimate interfacial interactions were prepared using a hydrothermal method for efficient photocatalytic degradation of various contaminants, including MB, RhB, MG, TC, and AMX upon visible light. The results showed that under visible-light exposure, the TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C-2 photocatalyst had excellent degradation efficiency for MB (99.3 % in 180 min), RhB (97 % in 210 min), MG (95 % in 180 min), TC (98 % in 30 min), and AMX (99.7 % in 120 min). Such outstanding performance was attributed to the optimal anchoring of Bi24O31Br10 and C-Dots on the surface of TOVs, the quantum dot size of the resultant photocatalyst, increased surface area, low charge transmission resistance, the effective presence of carbon dots with mediator properties, and most importantly, developed S-scheme heterojunction system, which suppressed e–/h+ pair recombination, as confirmed by EIS and PL analyses. This research provides a new perspective on developing a highly effective TiO2-based biocompatible photocatalyst for alleviating water contamination. It is anticipated that the newly developed S-scheme TOVs/Bi24O31Br10/C heterojunction photocatalysts could display potential applications for various applications, including generating fuels via CO2 reduction, facilitating hydrogen evolution, and disinfection of microorganisms.

Salmanzadeh-Jamadi Zahra: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Habibi-Yangjeh Aziz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Khataee Alireza: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Moslemi-Eilanlou Maryam: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are extremely grateful to University of Mohaghegh Ardabili and University of Tabriz for supporting and providing facilities.

Huang, Confined growth of Cu2O quantum dots on oxygen vacancies mediated Bi24O31Br10 nanosheets for efficient tetracycline hydrochloride photodegradation driving by S-scheme mechanism, Journal of Alloys and Compounds 1010 (2025) 177619.
